BIZ-OMICS · AQA A-Level Economics · Microeconomics

The Market Mechanism, Market Failure and Government Intervention

How the price mechanism allocates resources, where and why it breaks down, and what governments can (and can't) do about it
The Price Mechanism · Public Goods & Externalities · Merit & Demerit Goods · Competition Policy · Nationalisation, Privatisation & Regulation · Government Intervention & Government Failure

1

The Rationing Function of Price

In a market economy, price performs three distinct but interrelated functions. The first is the rationing function: price allocates a scarce good among the many people who would like to have it.

Rationing Function of Price
The role of price in allocating a limited quantity of a good or service among competing potential buyers, ensuring that the good goes to those willing and able to pay the market price, without requiring any central authority to decide who receives it.
1
A good becomes scarcer relative to demand

For example, a poor harvest reduces the supply of wheat (companion Price Determination guide, Section 11).

2
Price rises in response to the resulting shortage

At the original price, quantity demanded now exceeds quantity supplied (companion Price Determination guide, Section 17).

3
The higher price rations the now-scarcer good among buyers

Only those willing and able to pay the new, higher price continue to purchase it — quantity demanded contracts back to match the reduced quantity supplied, without anyone needing to decide by administrative rule who "deserves" the wheat.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Rationing via Price During Supply Shocks
Following supply disruptions to specific food items (for example, the well-publicised UK shortage of fresh salad vegetables in early 2023, caused by poor harvests in southern Europe and North Africa), retailers who allowed prices to rise saw quantity demanded fall to match the reduced quantity available — price performed the rationing function automatically. Where some retailers instead imposed purchase limits per customer rather than raising prices, they were substituting an administrative rationing mechanism for the price mechanism, precisely the alternative examined in Section 5.
2

The Incentive Function of Price

The second function of price is the incentive function: price changes provide a direct financial incentive for economic agents to change their behaviour, exactly as covered in the companion BIZ-OMICS Individual Economic Decision Making guide, Section 1.

Incentive Function of Price
The role of a change in price in encouraging rational, self-interested economic agents to alter their behaviour — a higher price incentivises producers to supply more and consumers to demand less; a lower price does the reverse.
Price ChangeIncentive for ProducersIncentive for Consumers
Price risesHigher potential profit incentivises firms to expand output (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 11) and encourages new firms to enter the market.Higher cost incentivises consumers to reduce quantity demanded, switch to substitutes, or exit the market entirely.
Price fallsLower potential profit incentivises firms to reduce output, or exit the market if price falls below average cost (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 10).Lower cost incentivises consumers to increase quantity demanded.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Electric Vehicle Adoption
Rising petrol and diesel prices, alongside falling battery costs, have altered the relative price incentive facing UK motorists — incentivising a growing share of new car buyers to switch towards electric vehicles. On the supply side, the same shifting price incentives have encouraged car manufacturers to redirect investment towards electric vehicle production, precisely the incentive function operating simultaneously on both sides of the market.
3

The Signalling Function of Price

The third function of price is the signalling function: price changes communicate information about the relative scarcity or abundance of a good throughout the economy, without requiring any single person or authority to gather and process that information centrally.

Signalling Function of Price
The role of a price change in conveying information to buyers and sellers throughout the economy about the changing conditions of demand and supply for a good, allowing decentralised decision-making without any central coordination.
Why the Signalling Function Is Distinct from the Incentive Function: the two are closely related but conceptually separate — the signal is the information a price change conveys (e.g. "this good has become scarcer"), while the incentive is the behavioural response that information then motivates. A rising price signals scarcity to every participant in the market simultaneously, and it is that same rising price which then incentivises the appropriate response (more supply, less demand) — no market participant needs to know why the price has risen for the signal to work.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Global Commodity Price Signals
A rise in the world price of lithium (a key input for batteries) signals to mining companies worldwide that lithium has become more valuable relative to the cost of extracting it, without any single body needing to identify or communicate the underlying cause (e.g. rising global electric vehicle demand). Mining firms across many different countries can each respond to this single price signal by increasing exploration and extraction, entirely independently of one another, illustrating how the price mechanism coordinates decisions across a global market without central planning.
4

Price and the What, How & For Whom Questions

Together, the rationing, incentive, and signalling functions of price (Sections 1–3) answer the three fundamental economic questions every economy must resolve: what to produce, how to produce it, and for whom it should be produced.

Fundamental QuestionHow the Price Mechanism Answers It
What to produce?Firms produce the goods for which consumers are willing to pay a price that generates profit (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Sections 22–23) — rising prices and profit signal (Section 3) where consumer demand is strongest, incentivising (Section 2) production to shift towards those goods.
How to produce it?Firms choose the least-cost combination of factors of production (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 12), incentivised by relative factor prices to use relatively cheaper inputs and economise on relatively expensive ones.
For whom to produce it?The rationing function (Section 1) allocates output to those willing and able to pay the market price — ultimately reflecting the underlying distribution of income and wealth across the economy (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide).
Why "For Whom" Is the Most Politically Contested Question: because the price mechanism answers "for whom" by reference to willingness and ability to pay, it directly links market outcomes to the existing distribution of income and wealth — this is precisely why critics of relying solely on the price mechanism often focus on this third question, and why government intervention (explored across the remainder of this chapter) frequently aims to alter who ultimately receives a good, not just how much of it is produced.
5

Advantages & Disadvantages of the Price Mechanism

Relying on the price mechanism to allocate resources carries genuine, well-established advantages, but also significant limitations that motivate much of the government intervention examined later in this chapter.

Advantages of the Price Mechanism

  • Highly efficient at processing and transmitting vast amounts of decentralised information (Section 3) without requiring any central planning authority.
  • Automatically adjusts to changing conditions of scarcity (Section 1), without the delays associated with administrative decision-making.
  • Provides continuous incentives (Section 2) for firms to innovate, reduce costs, and respond to changing consumer preferences.
  • Is impersonal and anonymous — it does not require buyers or sellers to know or trust one another, nor does it require any judgement about who is more "deserving" of a good.

Disadvantages of the Price Mechanism

  • Allocates goods according to ability to pay, not according to need — the price mechanism alone can leave those with very low income unable to access even essential goods (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Sections 14–17).
  • Fails to account for externalities (Sections 14–18), leading to systematic over- or under-production relative to the social optimum.
  • Cannot easily provide public goods at all (Sections 9–13), due to the free-rider problem.
  • Assumes perfect information and competitive markets, neither of which reliably holds in practice (Sections 19–20).
Assessing the "Impersonal" Nature of the Price Mechanism: the specification specifically asks students to assess the view that the price mechanism is an impersonal method of allocating resources. This impersonality can be viewed as a genuine strength — it allocates resources without prejudice, favouritism, or discrimination based on who a buyer is — but it can equally be viewed as a weakness, since it is entirely indifferent to genuine need, and can allocate a scarce good (for example, a life-saving medicine) to a wealthy buyer who simply wants it, ahead of a poorer buyer who urgently needs it. Which framing is more persuasive is, again, ultimately a value judgement (companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 13).
6

Extending Markets into New Areas

A distinctive and important line of enquiry in this topic concerns what happens when the price mechanism is extended into fields of human activity not traditionally organised through markets — the specification's own example of a market for blood illustrates this vividly.

Why Introducing a Price Can Change the Nature of a Transaction: extending the price mechanism into a new area does not simply add a price tag to an otherwise unchanged activity — it can fundamentally alter the incentives and motivations involved, and even change what is actually being exchanged.
★ CLASSIC EXAMPLE: Titmuss and the Market for Blood
As introduced in the companion BIZ-OMICS Individual Economic Decision Making guide (Section 15), economist Richard Titmuss compared the UK's voluntary, unpaid blood donation system with paid blood markets used in parts of the United States, and found that paying for blood can actually reduce total blood supply. The reasoning: unpaid donation is motivated by altruism, and converting it into a paid transaction can crowd out that altruistic motivation, turning a "gift" into an ordinary commercial exchange that many people find far less rewarding to participate in — and one that can also attract donors motivated to conceal risk factors (such as recent illness) in order to be paid, potentially reducing blood safety as well as supply.
ActivityHow a Market Might Change Its Nature
Blood and organ donationConverts an altruistic gift into a commercial transaction, potentially reducing supply (Titmuss) and raising serious ethical concerns about exploitation of low-income "sellers."
AdoptionAlmost universally prohibited from operating as a market, reflecting a strong value judgement that children should not be treated as tradeable commodities, regardless of any efficiency argument for doing so.
Water in developing countriesPrivatising water supply can improve investment and efficiency (Section 26), but can also mean the very poorest households are priced out of a good essential for survival, changing water from a shared right into a good rationed by ability to pay.
Congestion chargingIntroducing a price for road use (companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide) changes driving from a "free," first-come-first-served activity into one rationed by willingness to pay — efficient in reducing congestion, but raising equity questions about who can still afford to drive.
Exam Application: when assessing whether introducing a market into a new field of activity is desirable, always consider both the efficiency argument (does a price improve the rationing, incentive, and signalling functions relative to the status quo?) and the potential change in the fundamental nature of the activity itself (does paying for something change why people do it, or what is actually being exchanged?) — the strongest answers explicitly separate these two distinct lines of reasoning rather than treating "the market is more efficient" as automatically settling the question.
7

What Is Market Failure? Complete vs Partial

Market failure occurs whenever the free operation of a market leads to a misallocation of resources relative to the socially optimal outcome — but it is important to distinguish between two quite different degrees of failure.

Market Failure
A situation in which the free market mechanism, left to itself, results in an allocation of resources that does not maximise society's overall welfare — resources are misallocated relative to what would be socially optimal.
Complete Market Failure (Missing Market)
A situation in which no market exists at all for a good or service, even though it may be genuinely valued by society — the market mechanism fails entirely to provide any quantity of the good.
Partial Market Failure
A situation in which a market does exist and does produce and exchange a good, but at a price and/or quantity that misallocates resources relative to the social optimum — the market operates, but imperfectly.
TypeExampleWhy It Fits This Category
Complete market failureNational defence (Section 9)No private firm can profitably sell national defence to individual citizens, since it is impossible to exclude non-payers from its benefits — the market for it simply does not exist without government provision.
Partial market failureCigarettes (Section 18)A market for cigarettes clearly exists and functions, but because smokers do not bear the full social cost of their consumption (secondhand smoke, NHS treatment costs), the market produces and consumes more cigarettes than is socially optimal.
Why This Distinction Matters for Policy: complete market failure generally requires government to step in and provide the good directly (or fund its provision), since there is no market mechanism to correct — while partial market failure can often be addressed by correcting the price signal within an existing market (through taxes, subsidies, or regulation, Sections 29–32) rather than replacing the market altogether. Confusing the two leads to proposing the wrong type of policy solution.
8

Causes of Market Failure: A Roadmap

The specification identifies seven distinct causes of market failure, each examined in its own dedicated section later in this chapter. This section provides an overview map before the detailed treatment begins.

Cause of Market FailureBrief DescriptionCovered In
Public goodsGoods that are non-rival and non-excludable, meaning the free market will typically fail to provide them at all.Sections 9–13
Negative externalitiesCosts imposed on third parties not reflected in the market price, leading to over-production/over-consumption.Sections 14–15, 18
Positive externalitiesBenefits enjoyed by third parties not reflected in the market price, leading to under-production/under-consumption.Sections 14, 16–17
Merit and demerit goodsGoods whose true value to the consumer is misjudged due to imperfect information, alongside positive/negative consumption externalities.Sections 17–18
Monopoly and other market imperfectionsMarket power allows firms to restrict output and raise price above the competitive level.Section 20
Immobility of factors of productionFactors cannot move freely to their most valued use, leaving resources unemployed or misallocated.Section 21
Inequality in the distribution of income and wealthAn unequal distribution can itself misallocate resources, and can be both a cause and consequence of other market failures.Section 22
Exam Application: whenever a question asks you to identify the cause of market failure in a given scenario, work through this list systematically rather than guessing — many real-world scenarios actually combine more than one cause simultaneously (for example, cigarette consumption involves both a negative consumption externality and imperfect information about long-run health risks, Section 18), and identifying every relevant cause is usually separately credited.
9

Public Goods: Non-Rivalry & Non-Excludability

A pure public good is defined by two specific characteristics that, together, mean the free market will typically fail to provide it at all — a clear case of complete market failure (Section 7).

Non-Rivalry
One person's consumption of a good does not reduce the quantity or quality available for anyone else to consume — unlike a private good, where one person eating a sandwich means nobody else can eat that same sandwich.
Non-Excludability
It is impossible, or prohibitively costly, to prevent anyone from consuming the good once it has been provided, whether or not they have paid for it.
Pure Public Good
A good that is both non-rival and non-excludable — the combination of these two characteristics is what makes pure public goods a distinct and especially severe category of market failure.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: National Defence
National defence is the standard textbook example of a pure public good. It is non-rival: one additional citizen being protected by the armed forces does not reduce the protection available to any other citizen. It is non-excludable: it is impossible for the government to protect only fee-paying citizens while leaving non-payers physically exposed to invasion or attack. Because no private firm could profitably charge for a good no one can be excluded from, and because one person's enjoyment of the good costs nothing in terms of others' enjoyment, the free market would provide zero national defence if left entirely to itself — a clear case of complete market failure (Section 7).
Further Examples of (Near-)Pure Public GoodsWhy
Flood defencesProtecting one household in a flood-prone area does not reduce the flood protection available to neighbouring households, and it is generally impractical to exclude non-payers from the benefit of a sea wall or flood barrier.
Street lightingOne pedestrian benefiting from a lit street does not dim the light for anyone else, and it is not practically possible to switch the light off selectively for non-payers as they pass beneath it.
Lighthouses (the classic historical example)A lighthouse's warning beam benefits every ship in the vicinity simultaneously (non-rival), and it is essentially impossible to prevent any specific non-paying ship from seeing and benefiting from the light (non-excludable).
10

Public vs Private vs Quasi-Public Goods

Few real-world goods sit at the extreme ends of pure public and pure private — many instead fall somewhere in between, described as quasi-public goods.

Private Good
A good that is both rival (one person's consumption reduces what is available to others) and excludable (non-payers can be prevented from consuming it) — the standard assumption underlying the ordinary demand and supply model.
Quasi-Public Good
A good that possesses some, but not all, of the characteristics of a pure public good — typically non-rival up to a point of congestion, or technically excludable but only at significant cost, sitting between the extremes of purely public and purely private goods.
GoodRivalry?Excludability?Classification
A slice of pizzaFully rivalFully excludablePure private good
National defenceNon-rivalNon-excludablePure public good
An uncongested motorwayNon-rival at low traffic levelsExcludable via tollsQuasi-public good — becomes rival once congestion sets in (companion BIZ-OMICS Price Determination guide territory on congestion)
A public parkNon-rival at normal visitor levels, rival if severely overcrowdedTechnically excludable (fencing, entry fees) but rarely enforcedQuasi-public good
Free-to-air terrestrial television (historically)Non-rivalHistorically non-excludableHistorically closer to a pure public good — until technological change altered this (Section 11)
Fig 1: The full classification of goods. Crossing excludability against rivalry produces four categories, not just two: private goods (excludable and rivalrous, e.g. food, clothes, cars); club goods (excludable but non-rivalrous, e.g. cinemas, private parks, satellite TV — a further, named example of the quasi-public goods above); common goods (non-excludable but rivalrous, e.g. fish, timber, coal — the exact structure behind the tragedy of the commons in Section 13); and pure public goods (non-excludable and non-rivalrous, e.g. air, national defence).
Why "Quasi-Public" Is the More Common Real-World Category: genuinely pure public goods (perfectly non-rival, perfectly non-excludable) are relatively rare in practice — most goods commonly discussed as "public goods" in everyday language (parks, roads, broadcasting) are more accurately quasi-public goods, retaining at least some private-good characteristics under certain conditions, such as congestion or the availability of new exclusion technology.
11

Technological Change & Excludability

Whether a good is excludable is not a fixed, permanent characteristic — new technology can convert a previously non-excludable good into an excludable one, fundamentally changing how it can be provided.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Television Broadcasting
Early terrestrial television broadcasting was effectively non-excludable: once a signal was broadcast over the airwaves, anyone with a receiver could pick it up, and there was no practical way to prevent non-payers from watching. This meant broadcasting was historically funded either by advertising or by a compulsory licence fee (as with the UK's BBC), rather than through a conventional market transaction, since a direct pay-per-view market was not technically feasible. The development of encrypted satellite and cable signals, and later digital subscription streaming platforms, introduced genuine excludability — broadcasters could now technically prevent non-subscribers from receiving the signal or accessing content at all. This transformed television from a good with quasi-public characteristics into an almost fully excludable private good, allowing services such as Sky and Netflix to operate as conventional subscription markets.
Why This Matters for the Case for Government Provision: the case for government funding or provision of a good (Sections 29–31) is generally strongest when non-excludability is a genuine, persistent technical constraint. Where technological change removes that constraint, as with television broadcasting, the original justification for state involvement or a compulsory licence fee weakens, and a stronger case emerges for allowing the price mechanism to operate through ordinary subscription markets instead — a live and ongoing debate in relation to the BBC licence fee's future funding model.
12

The Free-Rider Problem

Non-excludability (Section 9) creates a specific behavioural problem known as the free-rider problem, which explains precisely why private markets fail to provide public goods, even when those goods are genuinely valued by society as a whole.

Free-Rider Problem
The tendency for individuals to avoid paying for a non-excludable good, since they can enjoy its benefits regardless of whether they contribute towards its cost — if enough people reason this way, insufficient revenue is raised to cover the cost of provision, and the good may not be provided at all.
1
A good is non-excludable

Once provided, no one can be prevented from consuming its benefits, whether or not they have paid (Section 9).

2
Each rational, self-interested individual reasons that they can enjoy the good regardless of their own contribution

Consistent with the rational, self-interested agent described in the companion BIZ-OMICS Individual Economic Decision Making guide, Section 1, an individual has no private incentive to voluntarily pay, since their consumption is unaffected by whether they pay or not.

3
If most people reason this way, insufficient funding is raised to provide the good at all

Even though every individual might genuinely value the good, and total social benefit may vastly exceed total cost, the good remains unprovided by the private market — a market failure despite universally rational individual behaviour.

Fig 2: Underprovision of a public good due to the free-rider problem. MSB and MSC cross at Q*, the socially efficient quantity — since MSB > MSC at every quantity below Q*, society would clearly benefit from a positive quantity of the good being provided. Yet because no individual can be excluded from the benefit once it exists, the free-rider problem (Section 12) means the private market provides none of it at all: actual provision sits at Q=0, not Q*, however large the gap between total social benefit and total social cost.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Community Flood Defences
Suppose residents of a flood-prone coastal town would collectively benefit enormously from a new sea wall, far exceeding its construction cost. If the wall were funded by voluntary contributions, any individual resident has an incentive to wait and see whether enough of their neighbours contribute to fund it, since they would be protected either way once built (non-excludability) — if enough residents free-ride in this way, the wall is never built through voluntary contributions alone, even though building it would clearly be worthwhile for the community as a whole. This is precisely why flood defences are typically funded through compulsory general taxation rather than voluntary local contributions.
13

The Tragedy of the Commons

The tragedy of the commons describes a related but distinct problem, arising specifically where a resource is non-excludable but rival — unlike a pure public good, one person's use of the resource genuinely does reduce what remains for others.

Common Resource (Common Pool Resource)
A resource that is non-excludable (anyone can access and use it) but rival (one person's use depletes the amount available to others) — for example, fish stocks in international waters, or a shared grazing pasture.
Tragedy of the Commons
The tendency for a common resource to be overused and depleted beyond its sustainable level, because no individual user bears the full cost of their own use — each user has a private incentive to extract as much as possible before the resource runs out, since any resource they leave behind will simply be taken by someone else rather than preserved.
1
No one owns the resource, so no one has a private incentive to conserve it

Because the resource is non-excludable, any individual who restrains their own use does not thereby secure the resource for their own future benefit — a competitor can simply extract the portion they left behind.

2
Each individual user rationally extracts as much as possible in the short run

Given Step 1's logic, restraint by any single user achieves nothing for that user, so all rational users converge on maximum extraction.

3
The resource is depleted beyond its sustainable long-run level, harming all users (including future generations)

The very absence of ownership and exclusion that seemed harmless for a non-rival public good becomes catastrophic for a rival common resource, since overuse genuinely destroys value for everyone.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: North Sea Cod Overfishing
North Sea cod stocks collapsed dramatically from the late twentieth century onward, widely attributed to a classic tragedy of the commons — international waters are non-excludable (no single fishing fleet can prevent others from fishing there), while cod stocks are clearly rival (a fish caught by one trawler cannot be caught by another). Each fishing nation and vessel had a private incentive to catch as much as possible before rival fleets did so, since any fish left in the sea would simply be caught by someone else rather than preserved for the restrained fisher's own future benefit — leading to sustained overfishing and stock collapse, prompting EU and international fishing quota systems (Section 32) as a corrective response.
Why This Model Is Central to Environmental Economics: the specification specifically highlights the relevance of the tragedy of the commons to environmental market failures — the Earth's atmosphere (as a sink for greenhouse gas emissions), the world's oceans, and shared aquifers all display the same non-excludable-but-rival structure, making the tragedy of the commons one of the single most important models for understanding why unregulated markets systematically over-exploit shared natural resources, and why collective action (whether through government regulation, international agreement, or the property-rights solutions examined in Section 32) is generally required to prevent it.
14

Externalities & the Absence of Property Rights

An externality exists whenever there is a divergence between the private costs or benefits faced by those directly involved in a transaction, and the full social costs or benefits experienced by society as a whole, including third parties not directly involved.

Private Cost/Benefit
The cost or benefit experienced directly by the producer or consumer involved in a transaction.
Social Cost/Benefit
The total cost or benefit to society as a whole, equal to private cost/benefit plus any external cost/benefit imposed on or enjoyed by third parties (Social Cost = Private Cost + External Cost; Social Benefit = Private Benefit + External Benefit).
Externality
A cost or benefit resulting from an economic transaction that falls on a third party not directly involved in that transaction, and which is therefore not reflected in the market price.
Type of ExternalityRelationshipLikely Market Outcome
Negative production externalityMSC > MPCOver-production (Section 15)
Positive production externalityMSC < MPCUnder-production (Section 16)
Positive consumption externalityMSB > MPBUnder-consumption (Section 17)
Negative consumption externalityMSB < MPBOver-consumption (Section 18)
Why the Absence of Property Rights Is the Root Cause: externalities fundamentally arise because no one owns, or can enforce a price for, the affected third-party resource — no one owns "clean air," so a polluting factory faces no direct cost for degrading it; no one can charge a fee for the knowledge spillovers created by a firm's research, so the firm cannot capture the full social value of its own innovation. Where property rights can be clearly defined and enforced, the Coase theorem suggests that private bargaining between the affected parties can, in principle, resolve the externality without government intervention (Section 32 explores this further through pollution permits, which work by creating exactly this kind of missing property right).
15

Negative Externalities of Production

A negative externality of production occurs when the process of producing a good imposes a cost on third parties not reflected in the firm's own private costs, causing the free market to over-produce relative to the social optimum.

Fig 3: A negative production externality. Because the firm ignores the external cost (EC) it imposes on third parties, MSC lies above MPC. The market produces at QE (point S), where MPC meets MPB=MSB — beyond the socially optimal output Q1 (point O), where MSC meets MPB=MSB. The vertical gap between MPC and MSC at QE is the external cost (EC); the triangle between points O, R and S is the resulting welfare loss.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Industrial Air Pollution
A steel manufacturing plant's private cost of production includes labour, energy, and raw materials, but does not include the cost of the air pollution it emits, which is borne by nearby residents through worse respiratory health and by society through the cost of treating pollution-related illness on the NHS. Because the firm's marginal private cost is lower than the true marginal social cost, the market produces more steel (and generates more pollution) than the socially optimal level, a textbook case of the welfare loss illustrated in Fig. 3.
16

Positive Externalities of Production

A positive externality of production occurs when the process of producing a good creates a benefit for third parties not reflected in the firm's own private costs, causing the free market to under-produce relative to the social optimum.

Fig 4: A positive production externality. Because the firm's activity generates benefits for third parties not captured in its own revenue, MSC lies below S=MPC. The market produces only Q, where MPC meets D=MPB=MSB — below the socially optimal output QOPT, where MSC meets demand. The shaded triangle is the welfare gain society forgoes by producing only Q rather than QOPT.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Beekeeping and Orchard Pollination
A beekeeper's private cost of production reflects hive maintenance and their own labour, and their private benefit is the honey they sell — but their bees also pollinate surrounding fruit orchards and wildflowers, a benefit to neighbouring farmers and the wider ecosystem that the beekeeper does not capture in the price of honey. Because the beekeeper's private benefit understates the true social benefit of their activity, the free market results in fewer beehives being kept than would be socially optimal, until the beekeeper is separately compensated (for example, through orchard-pollination contracts, a private solution echoing the property-rights logic of Section 14) or through government support.
★ FURTHER REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Research & Development Spillovers
A pharmaceutical firm's research into a new drug can generate knowledge spillovers that other firms and future researchers benefit from, even though the original firm cannot fully capture or charge for this wider benefit through patents alone — a classic positive production externality that helps explain why R&D tends to be under-provided by the free market relative to its true social value, and why governments frequently subsidise research directly (Section 30) or fund it through universities and public research institutions.
17

Merit Goods & Positive Externalities of Consumption

A merit good is a good that generates a positive externality in consumption, and whose true value to the consumer is also typically underestimated due to imperfect information — a combination that leads the free market to under-consume it relative to the social optimum.

Merit Good
A good that is judged (a value judgement, Section 14 of the companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide's framework applied here) to be more beneficial to a consumer than they themselves realise, and/or which generates positive externalities in consumption — typically leading to under-consumption if left to the free market.
Fig 5: A merit good. MSB lies above MPB, since consumers underestimate their own true benefit and/or ignore the positive externality (EB) their consumption generates for others. Left alone, the market settles at QE (point S), where MPC=MSC meets MPB — below the socially optimal quantity Q1 (point Q), where MPC=MSC meets MSB. The gap represents under-consumption, and the triangle between R, S and Q is the resulting welfare loss.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Education
A consumer choosing how much education to pursue may underestimate its full private benefit (better long-run career opportunities, health, and life satisfaction), and does not account at all for the substantial positive externalities their education creates for the wider economy — a more educated workforce raises productivity, innovation, and tax revenue, and is associated with lower crime rates, none of which the individual factors into their own choice. Left entirely to individual choice and private cost, the market would under-consume education relative to the socially optimal level, justifying substantial state involvement (Section 31) in its provision.
★ FURTHER REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Vaccination
An individual choosing whether to be vaccinated may weigh only their own private risk of illness against the small risk and inconvenience of the vaccine itself, without factoring in the substantial positive externality vaccination provides to others through reduced disease transmission (a case directly relevant to the free-rider logic of Section 12 as well, since an individual can benefit from "herd immunity" whether or not they personally contribute to it). This combination of under-valued private benefit and ignored positive externality is precisely why many countries subsidise or mandate vaccination rather than leaving uptake entirely to individual market choice.
18

Demerit Goods & Negative Externalities of Consumption

A demerit good is the mirror image of a merit good: it generates a negative externality in consumption, and its true harm to the consumer is also typically underestimated due to imperfect information — leading the free market to over-consume it relative to the social optimum.

Demerit Good
A good that is judged to be more harmful to a consumer than they themselves realise, and/or which generates negative externalities in consumption — typically leading to over-consumption if left to the free market.
Fig 6: A demerit good. MSB lies below D=MPB, since consumers overestimate their own true benefit and/or ignore the negative externality their consumption imposes on others. Left alone, the market settles at Q, where S=MPC=MSC meets MPB — above the socially optimal quantity QOPT, where MSC meets MSB. The gap represents over-consumption, and the shaded triangle is the resulting welfare loss.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Cigarette Smoking
A smoker's private benefit reflects the enjoyment and nicotine satisfaction they gain from smoking, but this typically overstates their true long-run benefit once the full future health costs are properly weighed (imperfect information about long-run risk), and entirely ignores the negative externality imposed on others through secondhand smoke and the cost to the NHS of treating smoking-related illness, funded by all taxpayers. This combination is precisely why cigarettes are treated as a demerit good and taxed heavily (Section 30) rather than left to the unregulated market.
★ FURTHER REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Excessive Alcohol Consumption
Beyond the direct health costs to the drinker (often underestimated at the time of consumption), excessive alcohol consumption generates well-documented negative externalities for third parties, including alcohol-related violence, drink-driving accidents harming other road users, and the wider cost to NHS accident and emergency services — all costs borne substantially by people other than the drinker themselves.
Two Important Boundaries the Specification Requires: first, whether a good is classified as a merit or demerit good always depends on a value judgement (some goods, such as private education or recreational drug use, are genuinely contested cases where reasonable people disagree). Second, and equally important: not every good with a positive or negative consumption externality is automatically a merit or demerit good — merit and demerit goods specifically add the additional element of imperfect information about the good's own private costs or benefits to the consumer, whereas some goods can generate consumption externalities (positive or negative) without any element of the consumer misjudging their own private benefit at all. Always check for both elements before applying the merit/demerit good label rather than the more general externality label.
19

Imperfect & Asymmetric Information

The price mechanism can only allocate resources efficiently if buyers and sellers have adequate information to make rational decisions. Where information is imperfect, or asymmetric between the two sides of a transaction, market failure results.

Imperfect Information
A situation in which buyers and/or sellers do not have all the relevant information needed to make a fully informed decision, making it difficult for economic agents to act rationally.
Asymmetric Information
A specific form of imperfect information in which one party to a transaction knows substantially more relevant information than the other party.
Cross-Reference to Prior BIZ-OMICS Material: imperfect and asymmetric information is examined in detail in the companion BIZ-OMICS Individual Economic Decision Making guide (Section 11), including the used car "lemons" problem and the PPI mis-selling scandal — those same examples apply directly here as a distinct cause of market failure, since imperfect information prevents the price mechanism from correctly reflecting the true value or risk of a good, leading to resources being misallocated relative to a world of perfect information.
★ FURTHER REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Food Labelling and Health Claims
Consumers frequently underestimate the sugar, salt, or fat content of processed foods marketed with health-conscious branding (such as "low fat" labels that compensate with high sugar content), leading to over-consumption relative to what fully informed consumers would choose — directly interacting with the demerit good analysis in Section 18, and a key reason UK policy has moved towards mandatory traffic-light nutritional labelling to reduce this specific information gap.
20

Monopoly & Monopoly Power as Market Failure

Market power allows a firm to restrict output and raise price above the level a competitive market would produce, creating a further distinct cause of market failure.

Cross-Reference to Prior BIZ-OMICS Material: the full diagrammatic and evaluative treatment of monopoly as a source of market failure — including the deadweight loss triangle, X-inefficiency, and the advantages/disadvantages of monopoly power — is covered in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Market Structures guide, Sections 22–24. That material applies directly here: a monopolist restricting output below the competitive level (companion Market Structures guide, Fig. 10) is a textbook case of the misallocation of resources that defines market failure (Section 7).
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Monopoly Power and Market Failure
As examined in the companion Market Structures guide, dominant firms such as national utility providers (e.g. water companies operating as regional natural monopolies) can restrict output and charge prices above the competitive level, creating a deadweight loss of economic welfare — precisely why such firms are subject to the price-cap regulation examined in Section 27 of this chapter, rather than being left entirely to market forces.
Other Market Imperfections: the specification also refers more broadly to "other market imperfections" beyond pure monopoly — this includes the oligopoly, monopolistic competition, and price discrimination material covered fully in the companion Market Structures guide, all of which can create some degree of allocative and/or productive inefficiency relative to the perfectly competitive benchmark.
21

Immobility of Factors of Production

Resources cannot always move freely to their most valued use, even when there is clear excess demand for them in one location or occupation and excess supply in another — a further distinct source of market failure.

Geographical Immobility
Difficulty moving a factor of production (typically labour) from one geographic area to another, due to barriers such as the cost of relocating, family and social ties, or regional differences in the cost of living.
Occupational Immobility
Difficulty moving a factor of production (typically labour) from one occupation to another, due to barriers such as a lack of transferable skills, the time and cost of retraining, or the specificity of existing qualifications.
1
A structural change reduces demand for labour in one region or industry

For example, the decline of UK coal mining and heavy manufacturing left many workers in former industrial areas without local employment.

2
Immobility prevents these workers from relocating or retraining to match demand elsewhere

High housing costs in economically thriving regions (geographical immobility) and a lack of digital or service-sector skills relevant to modern vacancies (occupational immobility) both act as barriers.

3
Resources remain misallocated — unemployed in declining areas, while unfilled vacancies persist elsewhere

This is a clear market failure: labour, a scarce resource, sits idle in one part of the economy while demand for it goes unmet in another, a misallocation the price mechanism alone has failed to correct.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Former UK Coalfield Communities
Many former coal mining communities in South Wales, Yorkshire, and the North East of England experienced persistently elevated unemployment for decades following pit closures in the 1980s and 1990s (companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 21), even as vacancies existed in service-sector and technology roles in other UK regions. Occupational immobility (mining-specific skills not transferring easily to office or digital roles) and geographical immobility (the cost and disruption of relocating, alongside strong community and family ties) both contributed to labour remaining unemployed locally rather than moving to where it was in demand — precisely the market failure this section describes.
22

Inequality as a Source of Market Failure

The final cause of market failure identified by the specification is inequality in the distribution of income and wealth itself — treated not simply as an equity concern, but as a genuine source of resource misallocation.

Cross-Reference to Prior BIZ-OMICS Material: the full treatment of why excessive inequality is both a cause and a consequence of market failure is covered in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 12, including the specific two-way reasoning chain linking inequality to under-consumption of merit goods such as education (Section 17 of this chapter) and the resulting reinforcement of the original inequality.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Inequality and Under-Consumption of Merit Goods
As set out in the companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, children from low-income households are statistically less likely to access the full benefits of education (Section 17 of this chapter) — a merit good already subject to under-consumption due to imperfect information and positive externalities. Inequality compounds this existing market failure, since the affected households may lack the resources to bridge even the gap between private and social benefit that better-off households can partially close through private tutoring or additional educational resources.
23

UK & EU Competition Policy

Given the market failure monopoly power can create (Section 20), governments maintain a dedicated body of competition policy aimed at preventing, detecting, and correcting anti-competitive behaviour.

Competition Policy
Government policy aimed at promoting competition and preventing anti-competitive behaviour by firms, typically covering the prevention of anti-competitive mergers, the prohibition of cartels and collusion, and action against the abuse of a dominant market position.
Area of Competition PolicyGeneral Principle
Merger controlProposed mergers and acquisitions above certain thresholds can be investigated and, if judged to substantially reduce competition, blocked or approved only subject to conditions (such as the sale of parts of the combined business).
Prohibition of cartels and collusionExplicit price-fixing, market-sharing, or output-restriction agreements between competing firms (companion Market Structures guide, Sections 16–17 and 20) are illegal and subject to substantial fines.
Abuse of a dominant positionFirms with substantial market power are prohibited from certain exploitative or exclusionary practices, such as predatory pricing intended to force rivals out of the market.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA)
The Competition and Markets Authority is the UK's principal competition regulator, responsible for investigating mergers, cartels, and abuses of market power, and for market studies into sectors of particular concern (such as its ongoing scrutiny of the "Big Four" audit market and UK grocery retail, both referenced in the companion Market Structures guide). The BA/Virgin Atlantic fuel surcharge cartel case (companion Market Structures guide, Section 17), in which the CMA's predecessor body fined British Airways approximately £270 million, remains one of the clearest illustrations of UK competition law in action.
★ EU EXAMPLE: European Commission Antitrust Fines Against Google
The European Commission has issued several multi-billion-euro fines against Google for abuse of its dominant market position, including a 2017 fine over favouring its own Google Shopping comparison service in search results, and a 2018 fine relating to its Android mobile operating system requiring manufacturers to pre-install Google's own apps. These cases illustrate the broad principle, without requiring detailed knowledge of specific EU statutes, that a dominant firm's ordinary competitive advantages can cross into illegal abuse when used specifically to exclude rivals rather than to compete on the merits.
24

Costs & Benefits of Competition Policy

Competition policy is not a costless corrective to market failure — it carries genuine costs of its own, which must be weighed against its benefits when evaluating its overall effectiveness.

Benefits of Competition Policy

  • Prevents or breaks up cartels, restoring competitive prices and output closer to the social optimum (companion Market Structures guide, Section 16).
  • Deters anti-competitive mergers that would otherwise concentrate market power and raise barriers to entry.
  • The credible threat of investigation and heavy fines can discourage anti-competitive behaviour even before it occurs.
  • Market studies and investigations can improve transparency and consumer understanding of a sector, even where they do not lead to formal enforcement action.

Costs of Competition Policy

  • Investigations are lengthy, costly, and resource-intensive for both the regulator and the firms involved, representing a real opportunity cost.
  • Risk of regulatory error — blocking a genuinely efficiency-enhancing merger, or failing to detect sophisticated collusion, are both possible and costly mistakes.
  • Compliance costs for legitimate businesses navigating complex competition law can reduce their competitiveness, particularly for smaller firms.
  • Fines and enforcement action can, in some circumstances, be passed on to consumers through higher prices elsewhere, partially undermining the policy's intended benefit.
Exam Application: the specification explicitly asks students to use real-world competition policy examples (such as those in Section 23) as contexts for evaluating the broader use of economic models to explore firm behaviour — a strong answer moves beyond simply describing a case and explicitly discusses what the case reveals about the underlying economic model being applied (for example, whether the theoretical deadweight loss of monopoly, companion Market Structures guide Fig. 10, was actually observed in practice, and whether the policy response was proportionate to it).
25

Public Ownership: For & Against

Public ownership (nationalisation) occurs where the government owns and operates a firm or industry directly, rather than leaving it in private hands — a policy option with a long and contested history in the UK.

Public Ownership (Nationalisation)
Government ownership and control of a firm or industry, with decisions made in principle according to public objectives (such as social welfare or strategic national interest) rather than private shareholder profit alone.

Arguments For Public Ownership

  • Removes the profit motive from provision of essential services, allowing decisions to be made according to social welfare rather than shareholder return.
  • Avoids duplication of infrastructure in natural monopoly industries (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 17), where a single provider is genuinely most efficient.
  • Can support broader strategic or social objectives, such as maintaining loss-making but socially valuable services (e.g. rural rail lines) that a purely profit-driven private firm might discontinue.
  • Profits (where they occur) accrue to the government, rather than to private shareholders, potentially funding other public spending.

Arguments Against Public Ownership

  • Removes competitive pressure that might otherwise drive efficiency, potentially leading to X-inefficiency (companion Market Structures guide, Section 24).
  • State-owned firms may be subject to political interference, with investment or pricing decisions influenced by electoral considerations rather than economic efficiency.
  • Access to public funding without the discipline of needing to attract private capital can weaken incentives for cost control.
  • Nationalised industries have historically faced criticism in the UK for weaker innovation and investment relative to comparable private firms.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The NHS and Post-War Nationalisation
The National Health Service, created in 1948 as part of the Attlee government's post-war welfare state (companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 21), remains one of the most enduring examples of public ownership in the UK, providing healthcare (a merit good, Section 17) largely free at the point of use. The same post-war era also saw the nationalisation of coal, steel, and rail, most of which were subsequently privatised from the 1980s onward (Section 26) — while more recent examples of public ownership include the partial nationalisation of RBS and Lloyds Banking Group following the 2008 financial crisis, and the temporary renationalisation of certain rail franchises that failed under private operation.
26

Privatisation: For & Against

Privatisation is the transfer of ownership of a firm or industry from the government to the private sector — the dominant UK policy direction from the 1980s onward, examined in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide's Thatcher case study.

Privatisation
The transfer of a state-owned firm or industry into private ownership, typically through a sale of shares to investors and the public.

Arguments For Privatisation

  • Exposes the firm to competitive and shareholder pressure to minimise costs and maximise efficiency, potentially reducing X-inefficiency.
  • Removes the firm's investment and financing decisions from short-term political pressures and electoral cycles.
  • Generates a one-off sale revenue for the government, which can fund other public spending or tax reductions.
  • Can improve access to private capital markets for funding investment, rather than competing with other government spending priorities for funds.

Arguments Against Privatisation

  • A privatised natural monopoly (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 17) can simply replace a public monopoly with a private one, without necessarily introducing genuine competition, requiring ongoing regulation (Section 27) to prevent abuse of market power.
  • Profit-maximising incentives may conflict with the provision of socially valuable but loss-making services.
  • Critics argue essential services (water, energy) should not be subject to profit extraction by shareholders, particularly where genuine competition cannot be introduced.
  • The one-off sale revenue is a temporary gain, potentially exchanged for a permanent loss of future public-sector income from a profitable asset.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The 1980s Privatisation Wave
British Telecom (1984), British Gas (1986), the regional water companies (1989), and the electricity generation and distribution industry (early 1990s) were all privatised during the Thatcher and Major governments, forming a core part of the wider 1980s policy shift examined in the companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 21. Royal Mail followed considerably later, in 2013. Because several of these industries (water, energy distribution) retain strong natural monopoly characteristics even in private ownership, each was accompanied by the creation of a dedicated economic regulator (Section 27) specifically to prevent the newly privatised firm from exploiting its market power.
27

Regulation & Deregulation: For & Against

Regulation and deregulation represent opposite policy directions for how closely government oversees and constrains market activity, each with distinct arguments in its favour.

Regulation
Rules imposed by government or a regulatory body that constrain the behaviour of firms in a market, such as price caps, quality standards, or restrictions on anti-competitive conduct.
Deregulation
The removal or significant reduction of government rules and restrictions governing a market, intended to increase competition and reduce compliance costs for firms.

Arguments For Regulation

  • Can directly constrain the abuse of monopoly power (Section 20) where a natural monopoly makes genuine competition impractical, without requiring public ownership.
  • Can enforce minimum quality, safety, or environmental standards that an unregulated market might otherwise fail to guarantee.
  • Price-cap regulation (such as the "RPI minus X" formula historically applied to UK privatised utilities) can mimic some competitive pressure on costs even for a monopoly provider.

Arguments For Deregulation

  • Removes compliance costs and administrative burdens that can act as a barrier to entry (companion Market Structures guide, Section 13), potentially increasing competition.
  • Can accelerate innovation and reduce prices where genuine competitive pressure, rather than regulatory rule-setting, more effectively disciplines firms.
  • Reduces the risk and cost of regulatory error or unnecessarily restrictive rules that outlive their original purpose.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Financial Deregulation — The 1986 "Big Bang"
The 1986 "Big Bang" deregulation of the London Stock Exchange, removing fixed minimum commissions and restrictive ownership rules for financial firms, is widely credited with transforming London into a globally competitive financial centre, but is also cited by critics as having contributed to the excessive risk-taking culture that played a role in the 2008 financial crisis (companion Labour Market guide, Section 4) — a clear illustration of how the same deregulatory policy can be evaluated very differently depending on the time horizon and outcome emphasised.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Bus Deregulation (1986)
The deregulation of local bus services outside London in 1986 allowed private operators to compete freely on most routes without requiring a local authority licence for each route, intended to increase competition and reduce costs. In practice, many areas saw competition concentrate into a small number of dominant regional operators rather than genuine widespread competition, and some less profitable routes were withdrawn entirely, illustrating that deregulation does not automatically guarantee the competitive outcome its supporters anticipate.
28

Regulatory Capture

A specific and important risk associated with regulation is regulatory capture, in which the regulator intended to constrain an industry in the public interest instead comes to act in the interest of the firms it is meant to regulate.

Regulatory Capture
A situation in which a regulatory body, established to act in the public interest, is instead influenced or dominated by the interests of the firms or industry it is meant to regulate, undermining the effectiveness of the regulation.
Mechanism of Regulatory CaptureExplanation
The "revolving door"Regulators frequently recruit staff with prior industry experience (valuable for genuine technical expertise), but the same individuals may later move into well-paid industry roles, creating an incentive to regulate leniently while in post.
Information asymmetry favouring the regulated firmFirms typically understand their own operations and costs far better than the regulator does, allowing them to influence the regulator's judgement through the information they choose to share.
Resource imbalanceLarge regulated firms can employ substantially more legal, technical, and lobbying resources than a regulator's own budget allows, tilting the practical balance of influence in negotiations and consultations.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Financial Regulation Before the 2008 Crisis
UK and US financial regulators in the years preceding the 2008 financial crisis were widely criticised, in retrospect, for having become too closely aligned with the banks they supervised — a light-touch regulatory approach was in part justified by regulators' close working relationships with, and reliance on, information provided by the banking industry itself, arguably reflecting a degree of regulatory capture that failed to prevent the excessive risk-taking examined in the companion Labour Market guide, Section 4.
Why Regulatory Capture Matters for the Overall Evaluation of Regulation: the possibility of regulatory capture means that regulation cannot simply be assumed to reliably deliver the public-interest outcome regulation is designed to achieve (Section 27) — this is a direct instance of the broader concept of government failure examined in Sections 33–34, and is one of the strongest arguments used by advocates of deregulation or genuine market competition over ongoing regulatory oversight.
29

Government Objectives & Methods of Intervention

The existence of market failure (Sections 7–22) provides the fundamental economic argument for government intervention in markets — but the specific form that intervention takes reflects a government's broader objectives, which extend beyond pure economic efficiency.

Government ObjectiveExplanation
Allocative efficiencyCorrecting market failure so that resources are allocated according to true social cost and benefit (companion Market Structures guide, Section 31).
EquityInfluencing the distribution of income and wealth (companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Sections 18–19), which may sometimes conflict with pure efficiency objectives.
Macroeconomic stabilitySupporting employment, growth, and price stability, which can shape the timing and design of microeconomic intervention.
Environmental sustainabilityAddressing environmental market failures such as the tragedy of the commons (Section 13) and negative production externalities (Section 15).
Public Expenditure
Government spending used to directly provide goods (Section 31) or to fund subsidies (Section 30) that correct under-provision relative to the social optimum.
Taxation
Used both to raise revenue for public expenditure and, through indirect taxes on specific goods (Section 30), to directly correct negative externalities by raising private cost towards social cost.
Regulation
Direct legal rules constraining the behaviour of firms and consumers (Sections 27, 31), used where price-based correction alone is judged insufficient or impractical.
Why Objectives Can Conflict: because governments pursue multiple objectives simultaneously, a single intervention can advance one objective while undermining another — a tax that corrects a negative externality (efficiency objective) may fall disproportionately on lower-income households if the taxed good takes up a larger share of their spending (equity objective), exactly the kind of trade-off examined more fully in Section 34's government failure discussion.
30

Indirect Taxation & Subsidies

Indirect taxes and subsidies are the principal price-based tools governments use to correct externalities (Sections 15–18), working by directly altering the price signal firms and consumers respond to.

Fig 7: Correcting a negative externality with an indirect tax, including tax incidence. A specific tax shifts supply from S1=MC to S2=S1+Tax, reducing quantity from QE to Qt and raising price from PE to PC. The tax burden splits between the two sides of the market: consumers pay the gap between PC and PE (burden of tax on consumers), while firms absorb the gap between PE and PF, the price they are left with after paying the tax (burden of tax on firms). The triangle A+B is the resulting welfare loss — the value of trades between Qt and QE that no longer take place.
What Determines How the Tax Burden Splits: exactly as with the incidence of any indirect tax (companion Price Determination guide, Section 6), the relative size of the consumer and firm burden in Fig. 7 depends on the price elasticity of demand and supply for the taxed good. Where demand is relatively inelastic (e.g. tobacco, alcohol), consumers absorb most of the tax through a higher price with little fall in quantity — precisely why demerit goods (Section 18) are common targets for this kind of tax, since it raises substantial revenue even as it only modestly reduces the over-consumption it is designed to address.
Fig 8: Correcting a positive externality with a subsidy. Because SMB lies above D=PMB, the market under-consumes at Qe. A subsidy shifts supply from S=PMC=SMC down to "S (after subsidy)", lowering the effective price and raising quantity to Qs, where the new supply curve meets PMB — closer to the socially optimal quantity where SMB would otherwise cross the original supply curve. The subsidy works by making the good cheaper to the consumer, directly increasing quantity demanded towards the social optimum rather than restricting it, the mirror image of the tax in Fig. 7.
PolicyCorrectsMechanismUK Example
Indirect (Pigouvian) taxNegative externalities (Sections 15, 18)Raises private cost towards social cost, shifting supply left and reducing quantity towards the social optimum.UK fuel duty; the Soft Drinks Industry Levy ("sugar tax," companion Individual Economic Decision Making guide, Section 1); tobacco duty.
SubsidyPositive externalities (Sections 16–17)Lowers private cost of provision, shifting supply right and increasing quantity towards the social optimum.Renewable energy subsidies; subsidised childcare; public funding for university research (companion Production, Costs and Revenue guide, Section 25).
Why the Tax/Subsidy Should Ideally Equal the External Cost/Benefit: a tax that is too small will fail to fully correct the over-production shown in Fig. 3, leaving some welfare loss unaddressed; a tax that is too large will overcorrect, creating a new welfare loss from under-production in the opposite direction. In practice, precisely measuring the size of an externality (e.g. the true monetary cost of a tonne of carbon emissions) is genuinely difficult, which is precisely the kind of information problem examined as a source of government failure in Section 33.
31

Price Controls, State Provision & Regulation

Beyond taxes and subsidies, governments have several further tools available to correct market failure, each suited to different circumstances.

Price Controls
Government-imposed maximum or minimum prices, overriding the price a free market would otherwise reach — a maximum price (price ceiling) set below equilibrium creates excess demand; a minimum price (price floor) set above equilibrium creates excess supply, exactly as with the national minimum wage examined in the companion Labour Market guide, Section 14.
State Provision
Government directly provides a good or service (rather than merely subsidising private provision), typically used for pure or near-pure public goods (Sections 9–10) where no private market would otherwise emerge.
ToolBest Suited ToUK Example
Maximum price (price ceiling)Making an essential good more affordable, though risking a shortage (excess demand) if set below equilibrium.Historical UK rent controls in some local contexts; energy price caps limiting the maximum unit price suppliers can charge domestic customers.
Minimum priceCorrecting under-pricing of a demerit good relative to its true social cost.Scotland's and England's minimum unit pricing for alcohol, setting a legal floor price per unit to discourage the cheapest, highest-strength products linked to problem drinking.
State provisionPure public goods where the free-rider problem (Section 12) prevents any private market from emerging.The NHS; state-funded primary and secondary education; the armed forces; flood defence infrastructure.
RegulationCorrecting information failure (Section 19) or restricting a demerit good/negative externality where a price-based tool alone is judged insufficient.The UK ban on smoking in enclosed public places (2007); mandatory seatbelt laws; food hygiene and safety standards.
Information provisionCorrecting the specific imperfect-information element of merit and demerit goods (Sections 17–18) directly at its source, rather than altering price or restricting choice.Public Health England/NHS anti-smoking and "Five a Day" campaigns; NHS vaccination awareness drives; mandatory nutritional labelling.
Fig 9: A price ceiling (maximum price) set below the equilibrium price Pe. At the price ceiling of 2.50, quantity supplied QS falls short of quantity demanded QD, creating a shortage — some consumers willing to pay the capped price cannot obtain the good. Producer surplus shrinks sharply (region F), while the triangle C+E is a deadweight loss: the value of trades between QS and equilibrium that no longer take place because the price ceiling prevents suppliers from being paid enough to produce that quantity.
Fig 10: A price floor (minimum price) set above the equilibrium price Pe. At the price floor Pf, quantity supplied Qs exceeds quantity demanded Qd, creating excess supply (a surplus) of unsold output. Consumer surplus shrinks (fewer units bought, at a higher price), while total welfare falls by the deadweight loss triangle between Qd and Qe.
Why Elasticity Determines the Size of the Shortage or Surplus: for a price control set a fixed distance from equilibrium, the size of the resulting shortage (price ceiling) or surplus (price floor) — and therefore the size of the deadweight loss — depends directly on the price elasticity of demand and supply (companion Price Determination guide, Sections 6–12). The more elastic demand and supply are, the further quantity demanded and quantity supplied diverge from equilibrium at the controlled price, producing a larger shortage or surplus and a larger deadweight loss triangle. Where demand and supply are both highly inelastic, the same price control produces only a small quantity divergence and a correspondingly small deadweight loss, even though the price gap from equilibrium is identical.
Elasticity ScenarioEffect on a Price CeilingEffect on a Price Floor
Inelastic demand and supplySmall shortage, small deadweight loss — quantity barely responds to the price gap.Small surplus, small deadweight loss.
Elastic demand and supplyLarge shortage, large deadweight loss — quantity responds sharply to the price gap.Large surplus, large deadweight loss.
Inelastic supply, elastic demandShortage driven mainly by the demand side rearing away from the low capped price.Surplus driven mainly by demand collapsing at the high floor price, while supply barely contracts.
Elastic supply, inelastic demandShortage driven mainly by suppliers withdrawing output at the low capped price.Surplus driven mainly by suppliers flooding the market at the attractive floor price, while demand barely falls.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Why Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol Targets an Inelastic-Supply Good
Alcohol supply is relatively inelastic in the short run (production capacity, licensing, and distillation/brewing lead times cannot adjust quickly), while demand for the very cheapest, highest-strength products among heavy drinkers is also relatively price inelastic. This combination means Scotland's minimum unit price produces a comparatively modest quantity distortion (and deadweight loss) relative to the direct public health benefit of raising the price floor specifically on the cheapest units — precisely the kind of elasticity-based justification examiners reward when evaluating why a minimum price was judged an effective tool for this specific market, rather than assuming any price floor is equally costly regardless of the elasticities involved.
Information Provision
Government-funded advertising, education, or mandatory labelling intended to correct the imperfect-information element of merit and demerit goods (Sections 17–18) directly, by giving consumers a more accurate picture of a good's true private costs or benefits — shifting MPB itself, rather than altering the price they face or restricting their choice.
Fig 11: Information provision for a demerit good. Information failure leads consumers to overestimate the benefit of a demerit good such as alcohol, tobacco, or fast food, so MPB1 sits above the true social benefit and consumption over-shoots to Q1. Government-provided accurate information about the good's true costs and risks shifts perceived private benefit down to MPB2, reducing consumption towards the socially efficient quantity Q* — correcting the over-consumption identified in Fig. 6, without needing to change price at all.
Fig 12: Information provision for a merit good. Information failure leads consumers to underestimate the benefit of a merit good such as education, vaccination, or preventive healthcare, so MPB1 sits below the true social benefit and consumption falls short at Q1. Government-provided accurate information about the good's true benefits shifts perceived private benefit up to MPB2, raising consumption towards the socially efficient quantity Q* — correcting the under-consumption identified in Fig. 5, again without needing to change price.
Why Information Provision Is a Distinct Tool from Regulation or Tax: unlike a tax or minimum price (which change the price consumers face) or a ban (which removes choice entirely), information provision works by correcting the consumer's own perception of MPB directly — it respects consumer choice while addressing the specific information-failure root cause identified in Section 19, making it a natural complement to, rather than a substitute for, the price-based and regulatory tools elsewhere in this section. In practice, government policy on demerit goods such as smoking typically combines all three approaches simultaneously (taxation, advertising restrictions/regulation, and public health information campaigns) rather than relying on any single tool alone.
Choosing Between Tools: the strongest evaluative answers do not simply list available tools, but explicitly compare them for a specific market failure — for example, a demerit good could in principle be addressed by a tax (Section 30, works through the price mechanism, but demand may be inelastic, companion Price Determination guide, Section 6, limiting its effectiveness), a minimum price (directly targets the very cheapest, most harmful products), or an outright ban (guarantees elimination of consumption, but risks creating a black market and removes all consumer choice, even for informed, low-risk consumers).
32

Property Rights & Pollution Permits

A further, distinctive approach to correcting externalities works not by taxing or subsidising a price directly, but by extending property rights to a previously unowned resource — directly addressing the root cause of externalities identified in Section 14.

Extension of Property Rights
Creating a legally enforceable, tradeable ownership right over a resource that was previously unowned (and therefore subject to externalities or the tragedy of the commons), allowing the resource to be bought, sold, and priced through a market.
Pollution (Tradeable) Permits
A specific application of extended property rights to pollution: government issues a limited, capped number of permits, each allowing the holder to emit a specified quantity of pollution; permits can then be bought and sold, creating a market price for the right to pollute and directly limiting total pollution to the capped amount.
1
Government sets a total cap on allowable pollution, at or near the socially optimal level

This directly targets the over-production shown in Fig. 3 by fixing the total quantity, rather than relying on a tax to indirectly shift firms towards the optimal quantity.

2
Permits equal to that cap are issued (or auctioned) and can be freely traded between firms

Firms that can reduce their pollution most cheaply have an incentive to do so and sell their spare permits, while firms facing high abatement costs can buy additional permits instead.

3
The overall pollution target is met at the lowest total cost to the economy

Because trading allows abatement to happen wherever it is cheapest, pollution permits can achieve a given environmental target more cost-effectively than a uniform tax or a uniform regulation applied identically to every firm regardless of its individual abatement cost.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS)
The EU Emissions Trading System, in which the UK participated before establishing its own linked UK ETS following Brexit, caps total carbon dioxide emissions from major industrial and power-generation sources and allows firms to trade allowances. A firm that invests in cleaner technology and reduces its emissions below its permitted allowance can sell its surplus permits to a firm facing higher abatement costs, directly creating the property-rights-based market described above for what was previously an unowned resource (the atmosphere's capacity to absorb emissions) — precisely the tragedy of the commons problem identified in Section 13, addressed through the creation of tradeable property rights rather than a tax or direct regulation.
Fishing Quotas as a Further Application: individual transferable fishing quotas, used in UK and EU fisheries management to address the North Sea cod overfishing example in Section 13, apply exactly the same logic — creating a tradeable, capped right to catch a specific quantity of fish converts a previously unowned common resource into something with defined property rights, directly targeting the tragedy of the commons at its structural root rather than relying solely on a tax or blanket ban.
33

Government Failure: Causes

Just as markets can fail (Section 7), so too can government intervention itself lead to a misallocation of resources — a phenomenon known as government failure, which means market failure alone does not automatically justify intervention.

Government Failure
A situation in which government intervention in the economy leads to a net misallocation of resources — the intervention itself creates inefficiency, potentially making the overall outcome worse than before the intervention, or worse than an alternative available policy.
Cause of Government FailureExplanation
Inadequate informationGovernments, like markets, may lack the precise information needed to correctly measure an externality or set an optimal tax, subsidy, or regulation (Section 30's tax-sizing problem) — an incorrectly calibrated intervention can overcorrect or undercorrect the original market failure.
Conflicting objectivesAs introduced in Section 29, a government pursuing efficiency, equity, and political objectives simultaneously may design an intervention that serves one objective at the expense of another, or serves none of them optimally.
