Administrative costsDesigning, implementing, monitoring, and enforcing any intervention consumes real resources (the salaries of regulators, the compliance costs imposed on firms) that must be weighed against the intervention's benefits.
Regulatory captureAs examined in Section 28, a regulator can come to serve the interests of the regulated industry rather than the public interest it was established to protect.
Unintended consequencesAn intervention can alter incentives in ways policymakers did not anticipate, creating new distortions or problems even while addressing the original one, as the examples in Section 34 illustrate.
Why Government Failure Does Not Mean "Do Nothing": the existence of government failure does not imply that government should never intervene — it means the case for any specific intervention must be assessed on its own merits, weighing the likely benefit of correcting the original market failure against the likely cost of the specific policy's own imperfections, exactly the evaluative approach modelled throughout this chapter (Sections 24, 26, 27).
34

Government Failure: Real-World Examples

Several well-documented episodes of UK and international policy illustrate the causes of government failure identified in Section 33 in concrete, real-world detail.

★ EXAMPLE: The EU Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) and Surplus Production
The EU's Common Agricultural Policy historically guaranteed minimum prices to European farmers well above the equilibrium market price (a price floor, Section 31), intended to support farm incomes and food security. The unintended consequence was chronic over-production — infamously described as "butter mountains" and "wine lakes" — as farmers responded rationally to the guaranteed above-market price by producing far more than consumers actually demanded, with the EU then forced to buy up and store (or destroy) the resulting surplus at further cost to taxpayers, a clear case of well-intentioned intervention creating a new, costly distortion.
★ EXAMPLE: Right to Buy and the Long-Run Reduction in Social Housing
As examined in the companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 21, the 1980 Right to Buy policy allowed council tenants to purchase their homes at a substantial discount, successfully expanding homeownership among many working-class households. An unintended long-run consequence, however, was a significant reduction in the available stock of social housing, since replacement building did not keep pace with sales — contributing to housing-cost pressures on poverty (companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 16) that the original policy did not anticipate.
★ EXAMPLE: The Universal Credit Five-Week Wait
As examined in the companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide, Section 19, Universal Credit was designed to simplify the UK benefits system and improve work incentives, but its built-in five-week wait for a first payment pushed some new claimants into debt or reliance on food banks — an unintended consequence of a policy specifically designed to solve a different problem (the complexity and poor work incentives of the previous six-benefit system), illustrating that even a well-evidenced policy response to a genuine problem can generate its own new difficulties.
★ EXAMPLE: HS2 Cost Overruns
The UK's HS2 high-speed rail project has experienced substantial cost overruns and repeated timeline extensions relative to its original budget and schedule, illustrating the administrative and information-related causes of government failure directly — large, long-term infrastructure projects are especially exposed to the difficulty of accurately forecasting costs and managing delivery over many years and changing governments, regardless of the underlying merit of the project itself.
Exam Application: as with market failure (Section 8), when asked to identify a cause of government failure in a given scenario, work through the categories in Section 33 systematically (information, conflicting objectives, administrative cost, regulatory capture, unintended consequences) — several of the examples above illustrate more than one cause simultaneously, and identifying each relevant cause explicitly is usually separately credited.
35

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From Externality to Corrective Policy

A firm's production imposes a cost on third parties not reflected in its own private costs (Section 14) → MSC exceeds MPC, and the free market produces beyond the socially optimal quantity (Section 15, Fig. 3) → this over-production represents a market failure, providing the economic case for government intervention (Section 29) → government imposes an indirect tax equal to the external cost, shifting supply left (Section 30, Fig. 7) → output falls towards the socially optimal quantity, provided the tax is correctly calibrated → if the tax is mis-sized, or firms respond in unanticipated ways (e.g. relocating production abroad to avoid the tax), the correction may be incomplete or generate new problems — a risk of government failure (Section 33) that must be weighed against the original market failure being addressed.

The Golden Chain: From Non-Excludability to the Case for State Provision

A good is non-rival and non-excludable (Section 9) → rational, self-interested individuals have no private incentive to pay voluntarily for it, since they cannot be excluded from its benefits regardless (Section 12's free-rider problem) → insufficient revenue is raised through voluntary means to fund provision, even though total social benefit may vastly exceed total cost → this is a complete market failure — a missing market (Section 7) — rather than a partial one, meaning price-based correction (taxes or subsidies) cannot work, since there is no market to correct → government must instead fund provision directly through compulsory general taxation (Section 31), converting a free-rider problem into a collectively funded public good.

Key Exam Tips

1

Always distinguish complete market failure (a missing market, requiring direct state provision) from partial market failure (an existing but distorted market, which taxes, subsidies, or regulation can correct) before recommending a policy response (Section 7).

2

For any externality diagram, always label MPC/MSC or MPB/MSB explicitly, mark both Qm and Qopt, and shade the welfare loss triangle between them — these are the specific elements examiners look for in diagram-based questions (Sections 15–18).

3

Remember that classifying a good as "merit" or "demerit" always requires a value judgement, and that not every good with a consumption externality automatically qualifies as one (Section 18) — check for the additional imperfect-information element before applying the label.

4

When comparing policy tools for a given market failure, explicitly compare at least two options (e.g. tax vs regulation, subsidy vs state provision) rather than describing only one — comparative evaluation is consistently the most rewarded skill in this topic (Section 31).

5

Always weigh the possibility of government failure (Section 33) against the market failure a policy is intended to correct — asserting that intervention will simply "fix" a market failure without considering its own potential costs is a frequently penalised omission.

6

Keep a mental list of one clear real-world example for each of the seven causes of market failure (Section 8) ready to deploy — public goods (national defence), negative production externalities (industrial pollution), positive production externalities (beekeeping/R&D), merit goods (education/vaccination), demerit goods (smoking/alcohol), monopoly (utilities), factor immobility (former coalfields), and inequality — since the specification explicitly expects real-world examples for every category.

BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | AQA A-Level Economics | The Market Mechanism, Market Failure and Government Intervention
